title: Automation and Ethics: A View from the Trenches
abstract: It is important that librarians know that unethical practices occasionally have occurred, and will occur in the future, in the field of library automation. Some vendors have “low‐balled” a bid—undersized a system—in order to make a sale. Some have bid undeliverable products. Libraries have occasionally “wired” a request for proposal—stated required specifications so that only one vendor can qualify. Consultants have occasionally compromised their objectivity and independence. Examples of unethical practices are described, as are procedures that will help identify and prevent those practices in the future.
authors: Wilson M. Stahl

title: State Standards Rise in Reading, Fall in Math.
abstract: Much ado has been made about setting high standards over past year. In his first major address on education policy, given just two months after he took oath of office, President Barack Obama put issue on national agenda. They ought stop for our kids, he said, adding that the solution to low test scores is not lowering standards--it's tougher, clearer In March 2010, Secretary of Education Arne Duncan accused educators of having lowered so they could meet requirements set by federal education law, No Child Left Behind (NCLB), which requires that all students be in reading and math by year 2014. Current conversations about creating a common national largely focus on substantive curriculum to be taught at various grade levels. Even more important, we submit, is each state's for student performance with respect to curriculum, as expressed through its proficiency standard. Curricula can be perfectly designed, but if proficiency bar is set very low, little is accomplished by setting content standards in first place. To see whether states are setting proficiency bars in such a way that they are lowballing expectations and have lowered for students in 4th- and 8th-grade reading and math, Education Next has used information from recently released 2009 National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) to evaluate empirically proficiency standards each state has established. This report is fourth in a series in which we periodically assess rigor of these standards (see Johnny Can Read ... in Some States, features, Summer 2005; Keeping an Eye on State features, Summer 2006; and Few Stales Set World-Class check facts, Summer 2008). The 2009 NAEP tests in reading and math were given to a representative sample of students in 4th and 8th grade in each state. NAEP, called the nation's report card, is managed by Department of Education's National Center for Education Statistics and is currently gold standard of assessments. Its proficiency is roughly equivalent to international established by those industrialized nations that are members of Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). If a state identifies no higher a percentage of students as being on its own tests than NAEP does, then state can be said to have set its standards at a world-class level. To ascertain objectively whether state standards are high or low, and whether they are rising or falling, we compare percentage of students deemed by each state with percentage as measured by NAEP. The state assessment data used in this report consist of those compiled in 2009 by 50 states and District of Columbia. States have strong incentives not to set world-class standards. If they do, more of their schools will be identified as failing under NCLB rules, and states will then be required to take corrective actions to bring students' performance up to higher standard. As a result, temptation for states to lowball expectations is substantial. Perhaps for this reason, a sharp disparity between NAEP standards and standards in most states has been identified in all of our previous reports. In 2009, situation improved in reading, but deteriorated further in math. Every state, for both reading and math (with exception of Massachusetts for math), deems more students proficient on its own assessments than NAEP does. The average difference is a startling 37 percentage points. In Figure 1, we provide a uniform ranking of rigor of state standards using same A to F scale used to grade students (see sidebar for specifics on methodology we used). title: Performance indicators contributing to success at the group and play-off stages of the 2019 Rugby World Cup
abstract: Performance indicators that contributed to success at the group and play-off stages of the 2019 Rugby World Cup were analysed using publicly available data obtained from the official tournament website using both a non-parametric statistical technique, Wilcoxon's signed rank test, and a decision rules technique from machine learning called RIPPER. Our statistical results found that ball carry effectiveness (percentage of ball carries that penetrated the opposition gain-line) and total metres gained (kick metres plus carry metres) were found to contribute to success at both stages of the tournament and that indicators that contributed to success during group stages (dominating possession, making more ball carries, making more passes, winning more rucks, and making less tackles) did not contribute to success at the play-off stage. Our results using RIPPER found that low ball carries and a low lineout success percentage jointly contributed to losing at the group stage, while winning a low number of rucks and carrying over the gain-line a sufficient number of times contributed to winning at the play-off stage of the tournament. The results emphasise the need for teams to adapt their playing strategies from group stage to the play-off stage at the tournament in order to be successful.
